query: is DNA loop-extrusion dependent on ATP

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adenosine Triphosphate -- Function -- DNA/RNA Synthesis
retrieved_docs.content: DNA and RNA synthesis requires ATP.  ATP is one of four nucleotide-triphosphate monomers that is necessary during RNA synthesis. DNA synthesis uses a similar mechanism, except in DNA synthesis, the ATP first becomes transformed by removing an oxygen atom from the sugar to yield deoxyribonucleotide, dATP. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Without the restoration of ATP, the Ca+ pump ceases to function, causing an influx of calcium ions. Free calcium levels are usually kept low inside the cytoplasm as calcium activates many cellular processes. With the increased free calcium now in the cell, it begins to interact with cellular enzymes that cause further membrane and nuclear damage. [2] Phospholipase, protease, endonuclease, and ATPase are all activated by increased cytoplasmic calcium. Phospholipase damages the plasma membrane by hydrolyzing phospholipids, resulting in whorled patterns called myelin figures. Protease results in proteolysis, breaking down proteins inside the cell and disrupting regular cellular activity. Endonuclease cleaves the phosphodiester bond in a polynucleotide chain, collapsing nuclear chromatin into clumps. ATPase breaks down any remaining ATP, quickening the loss of energy and leading to further damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adenosine Triphosphate -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: ATP is an excellent energy storage molecule to use as "currency" due to the phosphate groups that link through phosphodiester bonds. These bonds are high energy because of the associated electronegative charges exerting a repelling force between the phosphate groups. A significant quantity of energy remains stored within the phosphate-phosphate bonds. Through metabolic processes, ATP becomes hydrolyzed into ADP, or further to AMP, and free inorganic phosphate groups. The process of ATP hydrolysis to ADP is energetically favorable, yielding Gibbs-free energy of -7.3 cal/mol. [1] ATP must continuously undergo replenishment to fuel the ever-working cell. The routine intracellular concentration of ATP is 1 to 10 uM. [2] Many feedback mechanisms are in place to ensure the maintenance of a consistent ATP level in the cell. The enhancement or inhibition of ATP synthase is a common regulatory mechanism. For example, ATP inhibits phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK1) and pyruvate kinase, two key enzymes in glycolysis, effectively acting as a negative feedback loop to inhibit glucose breakdown when there is sufficient cellular ATP.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adenosine Triphosphate -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: ATP hydrolysis provides the energy needed for many essential processes in organisms and cells. These include intracellular signaling, DNA and RNA synthesis, Purinergic signaling, synaptic signaling, active transport, and muscle contraction. These topics are not an exhaustive list but include some of the vital roles ATP performs.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: To initiate the process of bacterial transcription, sigma and core together first binds to double-stranded DNA. [21] [2] C-terminal domains of the alpha subunits both interact with the UP elements, Region 4.2 interactions with the -35, and Region 3.0 interacts with the extended -10. [2] These interactions between core and promoter DNA are very unstable and short-lived, forming a product called the unstable closed complex (RPc). [21] The thinking is that the double-stranded DNA lies across the face of the RNAP. RPc quickly transitions to the stable open complex (RPo) where the DNA unzips, bends about 90 degrees, and forms a bubble from -11/12 to about +5 relative to the +1 transcriptional start site. [21] [22] Along with DNA changes, there are also major protein conformational changes within polymerase: Region 1.1 moves downstream to enter that beta and beta prime channel and beta prime clamp undergoes large conformational changes, allowing the downstream DNA to be fully secured. [22] Nucleotide triphosphates then enter through the secondary channel of the core, leading to the synthesis of RNA and forming a complex called the initiating complex (RPi). These RNA products are very short and are also known as abortives. Once the abortives reach a certain size, they can ultimately push Region 3.2 out of its current position within the RNA exit channel. [23] This change thus opens up the RNA exit channel, leading to the release of both the sigma factor as well as RNA. Core polymerase continues moving along the template stranded DNA until it reaches the termination site. In Rho-dependent termination, a factor called Rho is responsible for disrupting the complex of RNAP/RNA/template DNA while in Rho-independent termination, a loop forms at the end of the RNA molecule, allowing for RNAP to fall off. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: One notable structural phenomenon of DNA tertiary structure is supercoiling, or the coiling of the larger, already coiled DNA molecule. Specifically, in a DNA molecule that has its ends fixed, such as in the circular DNA found in prokaryotes or the smaller DNA segments that make up a larger chromosome in eukaryotes, separation of the individual strands of DNA during cellular processes causes the DNA to twist-up past the points of strand separation, leading to strain on the larger DNA structure. [9] This transient over-winding of the larger DNA structure when separating individual strands is known as positive supercoiling (Figure 5). Every cell has enzymes that keep DNA actively underwound to compensate for this, resulting in perpetual negative supercoiling, where the larger DNA structure coils in a left-handed fashion. This results in the strands of DNA needing less energy to be separated and keeps the molecule primed for easy separation in the events of transcription and DNA replication.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Replication -- Mechanism -- Elongation
retrieved_docs.content: Meanwhile, topoisomerases reduce the pressure on the winded portions and continue to open the DNA downstream to allow for elongation. They work by changing the amount of DNA coiling by adding negative supercoils or by unlinking DNA circles for prokaryotic DNA. Topoisomerase I relaxes the supercoil, and topoisomerase II adds negative supercoils. Topoisomerase is known as DNA gyrase in prokaryotes. Once the DNA strand is open, there needs to be a base for the DNA polymerase to bind and begin replication; this is provided by the primase. Primase adds short strands of RNA primers (9 to 12 pairs) onto the template to allow for DNA polymerase III or DNA polymerase alpha to bind and add nucleotides. DNA polymerase III (in prokaryotes) has 5’ => 3’ synthesis and 3’ => 5’ proofreading exonuclease. DNA polymerase alpha (in eukaryotes) is a complex that has the DNA primase which creates the RNA primer, and then the polymerase alpha itself elongates around 20 nucleotides and passes off to DNA polymerase epsilon or delta . DNA polymerase epsilon elongates and proofreads on the leading strand. DNA polymerase delta elongates and proofreads on the lagging strand. Because the strands run antiparallel, the polymerization mechanism is slightly different for both strands. The DNA polymerase runs in the 3’ => 5’ direction (therefore creating DNA in the 5’ => 3’ orientation), but only one DNA template strand, known as the leading strand, is in the proper orientation. Replication of the leading strand is simple because it is already 3’ => 5’ direction, the polymerase continuously adds complementary nucleotides to the primer towards the replication fork. For the lagging strand, which is 5’ => 3’, the new strand is synthesized in segments and discontinuously because the DNA polymerase can only read in the 3’ => 5’ direction. A new primer is added as the replication fork is further opened and the DNA polymerase delta builds the new DNA strand away from the replication fork, creating Okazaki fragments. Afterwards, another DNA polymerase replaces the RNA primers with DNA; this is done by DNA polymerase I in prokaryotes and DNA polymerase delta in eukaryotes. DNA ligase connects the fragments.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Molecular Level -- How is it made?
retrieved_docs.content: Prokaryotic RNA undergoes Rho-dependent or Rho-independent termination. In Rho-dependent termination, a Rho factor helicase binds to C-rich sites on the RNA, and ATP hydrolysis powers Rho to unwind the DNA-RNA complex and release the RNA transcript. [11] On the other hand, Rho-independent termination utilizes a hairpin loop that causes the RNA polymerase to stall and allows the RNA transcript to be released.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Replication and Transcription -- Molecular Level -- Replication
retrieved_docs.content: DNA has a double helical structure, where the two strands are joined together with hydrogen bonds between the complementary base pairs. Each strand is made up of nucleotides joined together by phosphodiester linkages. For the replication process to begin, the DNA helix must first unwind by removing the hydrogen bonds holding the two strands together. The unwinding process is accomplished by DNA helicase that often starts disconnecting the DNA in a region that is rich in adenine (A) and thymine (T). Since these bases have only two hydrogen bonds, instead of three, it is an ideal place for the helicase to start unwinding the DNA. Unwinding creates a replication fork that has a leading and a lagging strand, a result of the anti-parallel nature of the strands. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Quinolones -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Quinolones are bactericidal antibiotics that directly kill bacterial cells. They act on bacterial type II topoisomerases, DNA gyrase, and topoisomerase IV, inhibiting their function and converting them into toxic enzymes that produce permanent double-stranded breaks in the bacterial chromosome. DNA topoisomerases are essential for normal physiologic functions of the bacteria, such as DNA replication, transcription, recombination, and condensed DNA remodeling. They function by performing transient single- and double-stranded breaks, which help to facilitate their fundamental roles in removing torsional stress and knots in the bacterial chromosome that form during regular nucleic acid processes. Quinolone antibiotics stabilize the enzyme-DNA cleavage complexes by inhibiting DNA ligation. When the gyrase and topoisomerase IV create breaks in the bacterial chromosome to perform their physiological functions, it leads to fragmentation of the bacterial chromosome. When the DNA strand breaks overwhelm the cell’s ability to repair the DNA, it leads to cell death. [2] [3] [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Oxidative Phosphorylation -- Mechanism -- COMPLEX V
retrieved_docs.content: The fifth complex (complex V) is referred to as ATP synthase , for its role in the synthesis of ATP using the proton motive force. ATP synthase consists of numerous protein subunits, including F0 and F1. The F0 portion is hydrophobic, rooted in the phospholipid bilayer, and contains a channel for H+ ions to pass through to the F1 portion. The hydrophilic F1 portion (shaped like a stick attached to a cylinder) is the main catalytic site. It consists of a ring of rotating alpha and beta subunits surrounding a gamma subunit. H+ ions travel from the F0 portion to the F1 portion and, in turn, force the rotation of the F1 portion, which catalyzes the binding of ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi) to produce ATP. This process is the binding-change phenomenon. [13] [1] [2] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: ATP synthase, also called complex V, uses the ETC generated proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane to form ATP. ATP-synthase contains up of F0 and F1 subunits, which act as a rotational motor system. F0 is hydrophobic and embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It contains a proton corridor that is protonated and deprotonated repeatedly as H+ ions flow down the gradient from intermembrane space to matrix. The alternating ionization of F0 causes rotation, which alters the orientation of the F1 subunits. F1 is hydrophilic and faces the mitochondrial matrix. Conformational changes in F1 subunits catalyze the formation of ATP from ADP and Pi. For every 4 H+ ions, 1 ATP is produced. ATP-synthase can also be forced to run in reverse, consuming ATP to produce a hydrogen gradient, as is seen in some bacteria. [15] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial DNA Mutations -- Clinical Significance -- DNA Synthesis Inhibitor
retrieved_docs.content: Gram-negative bacterial resistance to fluoroquinolones includes the accumulation of substitution mutations in the coding regions for particular subunits of DNA topoisomerase II. Resistance can be enhanced further by efflux pump modification. [19] Ciprofloxacin targets only the parC subunit while other quinolones target one or more of these subunits. [20] For example, garenoxacin targets both DNA topoisomerases II and IV thus is less prone to resistance. Resistance to Garenoxacin requires both proteins to have resistance mutations. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: with a broad spectrum of individual drugs that may prevent infection (enfuvirtide, abacavir, delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, ibalizumab, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine, cabotegravir, fostemsavir, maraviroc, tenofovir disoproxil) and agents that treat established HIV disease (cabotegravir, fostemsavir, maraviroc). Integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir) block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration; NNRTIs (delavirdine, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine) bind a distinct site on reverse transcriptase, with rilpivirine contraindicated when co‑administered with the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole. NRTIs (abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine) act as chain terminators during viral DNA synthesis and form the backbone of many combination regimens. Additional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitory agents are classified as hypoxanthine derivatives, indicating that they belong to the chemical class of hypoxanthine and function as inhibitors of poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase. This classification highlights the structural relationship between PARP inhibitory compounds and the hypoxanthine scaffold, which is relevant for understanding their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction counseling in prevention strategies. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents demonstrates preventive activity against HIV acquisition, including delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil. Cabotegravir is recognized for therapeutic use in established HIV infection. These drugs are organized into pharmacologic classes that reflect distinct mechanisms of action: non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine, rilpivirine and nevirapine, which bind to a specific site on HIV reverse transcriptase; nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) include dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, acting as intracellularly phosphorylated chain terminators; and integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir—target viral DNA integration. Notable drug‑drug contraindications are identified within these classes: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, highlighting the need for careful medication reconciliation. Recognizing the relationships among needle‑sharing risk, the preventive and therapeutic roles of specific antiretrovirals, their class‑based mechanisms, and relevant contraindications is essential for constructing safe, effective HIV prophylaxis and treatment regimens and for optimizing patient counseling and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. These drugs belong to three principal pharmacologic classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Understanding the transmission risk posed by shared‑needle use together with the preventive and therapeutic properties of these NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors, and their class‑specific mechanisms, informs comprehensive strategies for HIV prevention, combination antiretroviral therapy, resistance mitigation and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: core antiretroviral class that inhibits HIV reverse transcriptase by chain termination. Representative NRTIs are abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil, and dideoxyadenine (to which abacavir is chemically related). Abacavir‑containing combinations (abacavir / lamivudine and abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine) are mapped to dideoxyadenine, reflecting this structural relationship. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme and prevent integration of viral DNA into the host genome. NNRTIs—including doravirine, delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine and rilpivirine—bind a distinct site on reverse transcriptase, inducing conformational changes that inhibit enzymatic activity. Doravirine has a documented contraindication with rifamycin antibiotics such as rifapentine due to clinically significant drug‑drug interactions. Additional agents with direct anti‑HIV activity

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1228
retrieved_docs.content: Delavirdin, efavirenz and nevirapin are all members of the pharmacologic class known as non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). NNRTIs act by binding to a specific allosteric site on the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, inducing conformational changes that inhibit viral DNA synthesis and thereby suppress HIV replication. Recognizing that delavirdin, efavirenz and nevirapin belong to this same drug class is important for clinicians because agents within the NNRTI class share similar mechanisms of action, resistance patterns, and potential side‑effect profiles, which influences the selection and management of antiretroviral therapy in patients living with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1220
retrieved_docs.content: Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a pharmacologic class that includes delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapin. All four agents share the same mechanism of action, binding to a distinct allosteric site on the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis without requiring intracellular phosphorylation. Because they act at the same enzymatic target, these drugs are grouped together for clinical decision‑making in combination antiretroviral therapy, allowing clinicians to select appropriate members of the class based on potency, resistance profile, tolerability and drug‑interaction considerations. Their classification as members of the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor class underscores their collective role in suppressing HIV replication and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 94
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and in HIV‑positive patients the immunosuppressive drug methotrexate sodium is contraindicated because it worsens immunosuppression and increases the risk of opportunistic infections. Prevention and treatment of HIV rely on three major antiretroviral classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine and rilpivirine – bind to a site on reverse transcriptase distinct from the nucleoside pocket; rilpivirine’s exposure is reduced by the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole, which is therefore contraindicated. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – act as chain‑terminating analogues incorporated into viral DNA. Integrase strand transfer inhibitors – raltegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and cabotegravir – block the HIV integrase enzyme and prevent viral DNA integration; cabotegravir also has therapeutic use in established infection. Together, NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors constitute the backbone of combination antiretroviral therapy, and knowledge of their pharmacologic classes, specific drug members, therapeutic roles, and contraindications (e.g., methotrexate sodium in HIV infection and pantoprazole with rilpivirine) is essential for optimizing patient care and reducing transmission risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Abacavir, an antiretroviral nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor used in HIV treatment, is mapped to dideoxyadenosine, indicating a direct chemical or functional correspondence between the two nucleoside analogues. This mapping highlights the structural similarity of abacavir to dideoxyadenosine, which can be relevant for understanding its mechanism of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and the design of related therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase inhibitors classified as anti‑infectives for systematic use comprise the antiretroviral agents dolutegravir and raltegravir, both of which are members of this drug class. These medications function by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA integration into the host genome and contributing to effective systemic treatment of HIV infection. Recognizing dolutegravir and raltegravir as members of the integrase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their shared mechanism of action within antiretroviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1024
retrieved_docs.content: Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) are a class of antiretroviral agents that inhibit the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme by binding to a distinct site, thereby preventing viral replication. The NNRTI class includes the drugs efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine, all of which share this mechanism of action. While NNRTIs are valuable components of combination HIV therapy, clinicians must consider potential drug‑drug interactions; for instance, the proton pump inhibitor pantoprazole is contraindicated with rilpivirine because it can impair rilpivirine absorption and reduce its therapeutic effectiveness. Awareness of these class relationships and contraindications is essential for optimizing patient care and achieving successful viral suppression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.
